title: fireground_crew_separation
description: Two firefighters on an interior search crew become separated in a smoke-filled structure. Self-rescue techniques, communication protocols, and crew leader decisions are tested.

timeline.title: Interior Search Underway
timeline.description: Two-person search crew conducting primary search in a smoke-filled two-storey residence. Zero visibility. Crew members in contact.

timeline.title: Separation Occurs
timeline.description: A floor collapse of debris severs physical contact between the crew members. Both lose tactile contact. Zero visibility remains.

timeline.title: Separation Confirmed
timeline.description: Both crew members realise they are separated. Firefighter B activates their PASS alarm. Firefighter A radios the IC.

timeline.title: Search Crew Leader Responds
timeline.description: IC directs a systematic location effort using PASS alarm, radio contact, and hose line navigation.

timeline.title: Firefighter B Located
timeline.description: Firefighter A locates Firefighter B using PASS alarm homing. Both exit together along the hose line.

timeline.title: Crew Exits Safely
timeline.description: Both crew members exit the structure. PAR confirmed. No injuries.

node-1.title: You Have Lost Contact with Your Partner
node-1.situation: You are Firefighter A, conducting a primary search in zero-visibility smoke on the second floor of a two-storey residence. You and your partner (Firefighter B) have been searching in physical contact — you had your hand on their shoulder. You crawled left around a door and when you reached back, your partner was gone. You call out — no response. You cannot see anything. You are on a hose line.
node-1.question: What is your immediate action upon realising you have lost contact with your partner?

options.label: A
options.text: Stay on the hose line — do not leave it. Call out your partner's name loudly. Radio the IC immediately: 'IC — Firefighter A. I have lost contact with my partner on the second floor. Request guidance.' Stay calm, conserve air, and wait for a response before moving.

options.label: B
options.text: Leave the hose line and search in the direction your partner should have gone — every second matters.

options.label: C
options.text: Activate your PASS alarm and wait for rescue — you may also be in danger.

node-2.title: Firefighter B — Separated and Disoriented
node-2.situation: You are now Firefighter B. When you lost contact with your partner, you continued searching briefly and are now disoriented — you have lost the hose line. Zero visibility. You do not know which direction the exit is. You have 150 bar remaining in your SCBA. Your low-air alarm has not activated yet.
node-2.question: What is your self-rescue action?

options.label: A
options.text: Stop moving. Activate your PASS alarm manually so your partner and any incoming rescue crew can home in on you. Immediately radio: 'IC — Firefighter B — I am separated and disoriented on the second floor. I do not have the hose line. I have approximately 150 bar.' Stay low and stay calm. Knock on the floor or wall periodically to create noise.

options.label: B
options.text: Crawl toward what you think is a wall and follow it — wall following will eventually lead to an exit.

options.label: C
options.text: Stand up and move toward any light — light means an opening and a possible exit.

node-3.title: IC Managing the Separation
node-3.situation: You are the incident commander. You have received radio calls from both Firefighter A (on the hose line, second floor) and Firefighter B (disoriented, off the hose line, second floor, PASS alarm active). Neither is in immediate life-safety distress — both have air and are communicating. Firefighter A can hear the PASS alarm. The RIT is staged at the entry. You must coordinate the situation.
node-3.question: How do you direct the resolution of the crew separation?

options.label: A
options.text: Direct Firefighter A to follow the PASS alarm sound toward Firefighter B while staying in contact with the hose line as long as possible. Instruct Firefighter B to stay stationary, keep the PASS alarm sounding, and keep radioing a status every 30 seconds. Position the RIT at the top of the stairs on standby. Monitor air status for both crew members continuously.

options.label: B
options.text: Deploy the RIT inside immediately to find Firefighter B — they are off the hose line and disoriented.

options.label: C
options.text: Order both firefighters to exit independently using whatever navigation they can — getting both out separately is the quickest solution.

node-4.title: Contact Re-Established
node-4.situation: You are Firefighter A. You have left the hose line briefly to follow the PASS alarm and have reached Firefighter B. You have physical contact with your partner. Firefighter B is calm and has 120 bar remaining. You have 100 bar remaining. Your low-air warning alarm has just activated. The hose line is approximately 4 metres behind you — you can find your way back.
node-4.question: How do you exit with your partner?

options.label: A
options.text: Radio IC: 'IC — Firefighter A. I have contact with Firefighter B. Both are mobile. My air is low — activating low-air protocol. Exiting now.' Maintain physical contact with your partner — hand on shoulder or wrist grip. Navigate back to the hose line immediately and follow it toward the exit, crawling low. Do not separate again.

options.label: B
options.text: Stay in place with your partner and wait for the RIT to escort you out — your air is low and movement will consume air faster.

options.label: C
options.text: Have Firefighter B lead the exit since they have more air — follow their lead.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: Outstanding performance by both crew members and the incident commander. Firefighter A correctly stayed on the hose line and immediately reported the separation. Firefighter B correctly stopped, activated their PASS alarm, and radioed their status rather than wandering. The IC appropriately directed a graduated response — homing on PASS rather than immediately deploying RIT. Both crew members maintained crew integrity throughout re-union and exit. No injuries. Air management was maintained within limits.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
